title: Metrology Meeting 2026-04-22
content: #Other Meeting notes for Metrology meeting on 2026-04-22 regarding the Calibration Probe Fixture project. Attendees include Kimberly, Matt, and David. Outcomes (from 2026-04-22) Junction cables (connect probe to instrument) are rated to 90°C. One cable is needed per bath. Kimberly refers to smaller baths as "baby baths" — these go to 220°C. Possibly 2 coming to Feynman; count not confirmed. Full range calibration cycle runs 14–20 times per year. The bath lab is open access — can run the bath independently to take the lip temperature measurement. Need to find a temperature gun in the facilities shop first. Pre-meeting prep Main blocker — resolve this first Why no metal, and is that rule flexible? Is it thermal conductivity affecting bath stability? Chemical compatibility with silicone oil or ethanol? Corrosion? Probably a combination, but need to understand the weighting before committing to polymer for the hot bath. Follow-up angle: if thermal conductivity is the concern, ask whether a polymer gasket or insulating interface between a metal structure and the bath wall would address it. Not proposing a specific design yet — just want to know if the door is open before closing off the material option entirely. If metal is off the table at 280°C — what is their reaction to Macor? Macor (glass-mica ceramic) is the next candidate if PEEK cannot handle the hot bath. Rated to ~1470°F, available on McMaster, but expensive (~$650/sheet) and limited stock sizes. Worth flagging so they understand the tradeoff. Current position: PEEK universal is the preferred direction for cold and mid-range baths. The only real question mark is the 280°C bath (Heat Source 4, 5014 medium). Not measuring lip temp, so need to either rule PEEK in from their experience or pick an alternate material for that one bath. Design decisions — need their input One universal fixture or two? Preference is universal, but if metal is only viable for the hot bath, that naturally splits. Ask what they want to work with day-to-day. Does the 280°C bath need to accommodate all probe types, or just the 5622-32? The 5622-32 is the only probe rated to 280°C — if that is the only probe they would run in Heat Source 4, the hot fixture can be much simpler. Confirm which probe models are actually in scope: 5606, 5622-32, 5622-05, 5622-10, 5623B. The 5 mm probes (5622-05?) are being phased out — confirm if still in scope or if the fixture can be designed around them. Things to collect from them Where to find probe spec sheets or dimensional drawings online (Fluke/Hart product pages, datasheets). Want a pointer, not for them to measure. Any existing documentation on bath lip and opening dimensions (maintenance records, equipment sheets) for the 4 Feynman baths. Is the CMC backup bath actually in scope? If it has a different lip profile, it changes the fixture design or requires adapters. Context to offer them Current material thinking: no metal was the direction, but revisiting it for the hot bath. For everything else, PEEK universal is the leading candidate — chemically resistant to silicone oil and ethanol, machinable, dimensionally stable, rated to ~250°C continuous. Materials already ruled out and why: Delrin (max ~90-120°C), PTFE (creep/deformation), Torlon (max 260°C, does not reach 280°C), Vespel (not findable through standard suppliers), PPS/Ryton (max ~220-240°C), Buna-N aramid gasket material (not structural, lower temp limit does not cover -80°C). Timeline: targeting May 2026, June acceptable. No CAD started yet — this conversation is what unblocks that. [!info]- Details & Notes Status: Meeting complete. Outcomes documented above. See also: Calibration Probe Fixture, Metrology, Kimberly Steinhauer, Matt Wahl, David Velazquez

title: Building Automation System
content: #Definitions A Building Automation System (BAS) is a network of interconnected systems designed to manage and control various functions in a building, including HVAC, lighting, and security. At Promega, the Facilities department uses the BAS to track, trend, and alarm HVAC, safety systems, and process equipment across campus buildings. It is governed by QSOP13.003. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: ECR, Particle Monitoring System, Feynman

title: CAPA
content: #Definitions CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) is a quality process at Promega involving in-depth investigation of product, process, and quality system issues. It draws on quality data from sources including nonconformances and complaints, and tracks actions through to completion with effectiveness assessments. A cross-functional CAPA Board made up of managers and directors from Development, Manufacturing/Operations, and QA oversees the process. It is governed by QP22 alongside Nonconformances. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Nonconformance, QA and Regulatory Affairs, Change Control

title: Change Control
content: #Definitions Change Control (CC) is a formal process at Promega for identifying, evaluating, approving, and implementing changes. It covers a wide scope including changes to facilities, utilities, equipment, products, processes, software, and documents. It is governed by QP24 (Rev 1). Change Control is a key step in deploying new or modified production parts and equipment, and is something I encounter in fill/finish engineering work. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Engineering Study, Validation, SOP, EPDM

title: Cleanroom

In the Feynman building, cleanrooms are used for fill/finish operations on the MA lines. The terms cleanroom and ECR (Environmentally Controlled Room) are often used interchangeably at Promega, though they may be technically distinct. [!info]- Details & Notes Unknowns: Whether cleanroom and ECR are technically the same classification or differ in some way at Promega. #unsure-or-needs-to-be-finished See also: ECR, Gowning, Feynman

title: Design Control
content: #Definitions Design Control is a formal engineering process at Promega structured into distinct phases with phase gates. It involves a cross-functional team including representatives from R&D, QA, and Manufacturing, with risk management applied throughout the product lifecycle. It is governed by QP14 (Rev 1). Design Control is distinct from a Design Review, which is a single checkpoint within a design process. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Design Review, Risk Assessment, Validation, Change Control

title: ECR
content: #Definitions An Environmentally Controlled Room (ECR) is a specially designed space where environmental parameters such as temperature, humidity, particulate contamination, and air pressure are precisely regulated. ECRs at Promega are governed by ISO 14644, which outlines requirements for air exchange rates, pressure differentials, particulate monitoring, and HEPA filtration. The Facilities department is responsible for maintaining, alarming, and coordinating testing for ECR areas. The terms ECR and Cleanroom are used interchangeably at Promega — there is no meaningful distinction between them in practice. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Cleanroom, Gowning, Feynman, Building Automation System, Particle Monitoring System

title: Electrophoresis
content: #Definitions Electrophoresis is a laboratory technique used to separate molecules — typically DNA, RNA, or proteins — based on their size and electrical charge. A sample is placed in a medium and an electrical current is applied, causing the molecules to migrate through the medium. Smaller molecules move faster than larger ones, so they separate out over time. The result is a pattern that can be read to identify or analyze the molecules present. At Promega, electrophoresis comes up in both product development and product use — the company makes products used in electrophoresis workflows, and uses electrophoresis techniques internally. Gel Electrophoresis Gel electrophoresis is the most common form of electrophoresis. Samples are loaded into wells cut into a slab of gel (typically agarose for DNA, or polyacrylamide for proteins) and an electric current is applied. The molecules migrate through the gel matrix at different rates depending on their size, producing distinct bands that can be visualized under UV light or with a stain. At Promega, gel electrophoresis trays are used to run gels — these are physical trays that hold the gel and provide the wells for loading samples. See Gel Electrophoresis Tray. Capillary Electrophoresis Capillary electrophoresis (CE) performs the same basic separation but inside a narrow capillary tube filled with a polymer solution rather than a flat gel slab. It offers higher resolution, faster run times, and automated detection compared to gel electrophoresis. The Spectrum device is a Promega capillary electrophoresis instrument used to analyze DNA — most commonly for forensics and paternity testing applications. Septa Mats are used with the Spectrum device to seal the plate during a run. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Spectrum, Septa Mats, Gel Electrophoresis Tray

title: ISA-88
content: #Definitions ISA-88 (also called S88) is an international standard for batch process control. It defines a common framework and terminology for how batch manufacturing systems should be structured — including the hierarchy of control layers from individual devices up through full recipes. The standard is widely used in pharmaceutical, biotech, and food manufacturing industries. At Promega, the Batch fermentation control system follows the ISA-88 model. The layered hierarchy of Control Modules, Equipment Modules, Phases, Operating Procedures, and Unit Procedures all come from this standard. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Batch, Fermentation

title: Lysis
content: #Definitions Lysis (verb: lyse) refers to the process of breaking open a cell by rupturing its membrane to release its internal contents — DNA, proteins, enzymes, or other molecules of interest. It is typically one of the first steps in any molecular biology workflow, since most assays and techniques require access to material that is otherwise locked inside the cell. Cells can be lysed in several ways depending on the application: chemical detergents, heat, mechanical force, or specialized lysis buffers formulated for specific cell types. For example, before running a PCR, the DNA must first be extracted from cells — lysis is how that extraction begins. Without it, the polymerase has nothing to work with. Promega produces a range of lysis reagents and buffers as part of their broader molecular biology product catalog. These are used by research labs as upstream steps before detection, amplification, or analysis workflows. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Polymerase Chain Reaction, Polymerase

title: Multiple Materials Request Spreadsheet
content: #Definitions The Multiple Materials Request Spreadsheet is an Excel-based form used to request SAP part numbers for 5 or more materials at once. It lives on SharePoint in the Plant Maintenance/FEEP area and is maintained by Nikki Masterson. For fewer than 5 parts, the Smartsheet-based Parts Order Form is used instead. The spreadsheet has five tabs, each corresponding to a different request type: Existing SAP ERSA Material, Existing SAP NLAG Material, New SAP ERSA Material, New SAP NLAG Material, and No SAP Material. The New SAP ERSA Material tab is the most relevant for adding newly machined or purchased parts to the system. Its columns are: SAP Material Description, Automated Reordering, Reorder Point (if Automated Reordering = Yes), Rounding Value (if Automated Reordering = Yes), Equipment BOM Information, Vendor Material Number, Order Quantity, Unit of Measure, Price per Unit, Storage Location, Quantity on Hand, and Storage Bin. Fields marked as required must be completed to submit the request; the remaining columns are optional SAP data updates. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Mixer Chuck See also: Parts Order Form, ERSA, NLAG, SAP, Nikki

title: Parts Process
content: #Definitions An Internally Produced Production Part (IPPP) is any component produced or manufactured internally by the Operations Engineering or Packaging Engineering departments with the intention of being permanently used in a Promega production environment. Prototype parts are explicitly out of scope. The full process for creating and deploying an IPPP is governed by SOP22698 #unsure-or-needs-to-be-finished (SOP number may also be SOP226898 — verify against the document control system). The parts process touches SAP, EPDM, and the Certificate of Compliance form at different stages. Getting a part number from the SAP Analyst is the first step before anything else happens. As of early 2026, that contact is Gary McCauley. Parts Process — Step by Step Step 1 — Part Setup Request a part number from the SAP Analyst (Gary McCauley as of early 2026). Nikki is also a contact for SAP part number requests. For fewer than 5 parts, use the Parts Order Form (Smartsheet) to submit the request. For 5 or more parts at once, use the Multiple Materials Request Spreadsheet instead. The Parts Order Form is available at: https://app.smartsheet.com/b/publish?EQBCT=ad110954f1e047ab85f66a46824875d7 For most fabricated parts, the request type is New SAP ERSA material. Print the completed form to PDF before submitting as a record. Key fields on the form: Requester Email: Ryan.meyer@promega.com Type of Request: New SAP ERSA material (most common) Approver: Claire Moll (Ali Heinemann is not currently an option as approver on this form) Material Description: 40-character hard limit — use the naming convention from QSOP23.007. Format is: Part Name, Use Case, Description/Material, Size, Part Number. Omit fields that don't apply. SAP Equipment BOM Information: list each SAP equipment number and BOM quantity (quantity = how many the equipment uses at one time, not production run size) Vendor: required — use Travis Beyer / Lamarr Shop for in-house machined parts Unit of Measure: EA (each) for most parts Building and Storage Location (SLOC): always required — do not assume File Upload: attach quote or datasheet if available; attach the Multiple Materials Request Spreadsheet if that route was used Comments: include project name and traceability context For questions on the form, contact Gary McCauley (gary.mccauley@promega.com). The form is managed through Smartsheet and approvals are routed automatically once submitted. Step 2 — Create the Engineering Drawing Create the drawing and save it to EPDM. Key checklist before the drawing is considered complete: Title block is correct and fully filled out All critical dimensions have appropriate tolerance ranges annotated Critical dimensions are identified (circles are used in SolidWorks for this) Revision block is present Drawing has a reviewer and approver — "Drawn By" never changes, but "Approved By" updates with every revision Proper drawing symbols and callouts are used (follow ASME Y14.5 where applicable) In EPDM, create a folder with the part number under the appropriate equipment folder. If the part doesn't fit under an existing equipment folder, it can go in the Custom Jigs_Fixtures folder. Step 3 — Produce the Part Make the part, or have it made. Options include: Make it yourself using available fabrication equipment Have it sourced externally Submit a request to the Lamarr Shop (request via Smartsheet) When possible, physically mark the part with the part number and revision level using indelible means. If marking directly on the part isn't feasible, use a labeled storage solution instead. Step 4 — Complete the Certificate of Compliance (CoC) The CoC must be completed before the part is put into service. Steps: Fill out all production information: quantity produced, date of production, who produced them, and where they are being deployed Verify all critical dimensions are within acceptable tolerance ranges on each part Ensure SAP BOMs are updated to reflect the new part If the part is used to determine acceptable quality limits, confirm a PM plan is set up in SAP Save the completed CoC to EPDM in the same folder as the drawing. The CoC filename must include the part or equipment number so it is easily searchable. Step 5 — Deploy the Part Once the CoC is uploaded to EPDM, the part can be turned over to the end user (Production). As the author/originator, it is your responsibility to remove any old parts being replaced. Confirm SAP BOMs are adjusted and any required PM plans are in place. [!info]- Details & Notes Unknowns: SOP number is uncertain — DOCX references SOP22698, PPTX filename references SOP226898. Verify against the document control system. #unsure-or-needs-to-be-finished Encountered in: Mixer Chuck (parts process is a key deliverable for this project) See also: SAP, EPDM, CoC, Lamarr, Gary McCauley, Change Control, Parts Order Form, Multiple Materials Request Spreadsheet, Nikki

title: Polymerase Chain Reaction
content: #Definitions Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) is a molecular biology technique used to rapidly amplify a specific segment of DNA, producing millions to billions of copies from a very small starting sample. It is one of the most foundational tools in modern biology and medicine. A PCR reaction works by cycling through three temperature-controlled steps repeatedly. First, denaturation at around 95°C separates double-stranded DNA into two single strands. Next, annealing at roughly 50–65°C allows short synthetic DNA sequences called primers to bind to the target region on each strand. Finally, extension at around 72°C allows a heat-stable enzyme called DNA polymerase — most commonly Taq polymerase — to synthesize a new complementary copy of each strand. Each cycle approximately doubles the amount of target DNA, so after 30–40 cycles the amplification is exponential. A full run typically takes one to three hours. PCR is used across a wide range of applications: genetic testing, disease diagnosis (including COVID-19 tests), forensic analysis, research cloning, and more. It is essentially indispensable to modern molecular biology. Promega is a major commercial supplier of PCR reagents and kits. Products include GoTaq and GoTaq Green mastermixes, Taq DNA Polymerase, dNTPs (the individual nucleotide building blocks), and specialized polymerases for high-fidelity or long-range PCR applications. Fill/finish operations in the Feynman building are likely involved in producing some of these reagents as final product for distribution to research labs worldwide. Before PCR can be performed, DNA must first be extracted from cells via lysis. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: GoTaq, Taq DNA Polymerase, Feynman, Polymerase, Lysis

title: Polymerase
content: #Definitions A polymerase is an enzyme that builds new chains of nucleic acids — either DNA or RNA — by linking together individual nucleotide building blocks. The name comes from "polymer" (a long repeating chain) combined with "-ase," the standard suffix for enzymes. There are two main types encountered in molecular biology. DNA polymerase copies DNA by reading an existing template strand and synthesizing a new complementary strand alongside it. RNA polymerase transcribes DNA into RNA, which is the first step of gene expression. In the context of PCR, it is DNA polymerase doing the work — specifically a heat-stable variant called Taq polymerase, which survives the high temperatures required during the denaturation step. Taq comes from a bacterium called Thermus aquaticus that naturally lives in hot springs. Promega sells multiple polymerase products, including Taq DNA Polymerase and higher-fidelity variants for applications that require more accurate copying. These are core to Promega's reagent catalog and are likely among the products filled in the Feynman building. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Polymerase Chain Reaction, Lysis, Feynman

title: Preventive Maintenance
content: #Definitions Preventive Maintenance (PM) is a scheduled maintenance process applied to production equipment at Promega. PMs are carried out on a regular basis to keep equipment in good working condition and prevent unplanned downtime. Because multiple systems are interdependent on the production floor, a PM on one piece of equipment can have downstream effects on others — for example, a KPA air pressure loss was noted as likely fallout from a recent PM. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: KPA, Operations Engineering

title: Promega Instrumentation
content: Promega develops and manufactures a range of scientific instruments used in molecular biology, forensics, and life sciences research. As a fill/finish engineer, the instruments are relevant because the cartridges, kits, and reagents produced on the production lines here — like the MaxAuto lines — are what end customers load into these instruments to do their work. Understanding what the instruments do gives context for why what we make matters. Two instruments I encounter most in this context are the Maxwell RSC and the Spectrum. The Maxwell® RSC is an automated nucleic acid extraction instrument. It uses prefilled cartridges — the kind filled and labeled on the MaxAuto lines — to purify DNA or RNA from a variety of sample types using magnetic bead-based separation. Customers use it in research, clinical, and food safety applications. The Spectrum is Promega's capillary electrophoresis instrument. It separates and detects fluorescently labeled DNA fragments, primarily short tandem repeat (STR) markers, and is used mainly in forensic laboratories for human identification. It supports 5-, 6-, and 8-color chemistry and can hold up to four 96-well plates simultaneously. #Definitions [!info]- Details & Notes Unknowns: Full range of Promega instruments not yet documented here. Other notable instruments include the GloMax® luminometer/plate reader family and the Maxprep® Liquid Handler. Not all instruments have a direct connection to fill/finish operations. See also: Maxwell RSC, Spectrum, MaxAuto, Electrophoresis

title: Promega
content: #Definitions Promega Corporation is a Madison, Wisconsin–based manufacturer of enzymes and other products for biotechnology and molecular biology with a portfolio covering the fields of genomics, protein analysis and expression, cellular analysis, drug discovery, and genetic identity. History Promega Corporation was founded by Bill Linton in 1978 to provide restriction enzymes for biotechnology. The company now offers more than 4,000 life science products used by scientists, researchers and life science and pharmaceutical companies. Promega has 1,601 employees. Revenue is approaching $450 million (USD) in 2019. The privately held company has branch offices in 16 countries and more than 50 global distributors serving 100 countries. Promega Corporation also established the first biotechnology joint venture in China (Sino-American Biotechnology Co. in 1985). The company has developed an on-site stocking system, which uses radio frequency identification (RFID) linked to the Internet to track and manage remote inventory. This resulted in the spin-off company Terso Solutions that specializes in the design and manufacturing of small RFID storage units. In February 2020, Foreign Policy reported that Promega had sold equipment to the Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps. In 2021, The New York Times reported that, despite bans, Promega equipment continued to be sold to police in Xinjiang. See also Usona Institute

title: Resistance Temperature Detector
content: #Definitions A Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) is a temperature sensor that works by measuring the change in electrical resistance of a material — typically platinum — as temperature changes. As temperature rises, resistance increases in a known, repeatable, and nearly linear way, allowing for precise temperature measurement. Platinum RTDs are preferred for industrial applications because platinum is stable, does not oxidize easily, and covers a wide temperature range (roughly -200°C to 850°C). RTDs offer higher accuracy and long-term stability compared to thermocouples, which is why they are increasingly used in industrial settings below 600°C. In my work, RTDs came up as the probe type being calibrated in the Calibration Probe Fixture project. [!info]- Details & Notes Common construction: Platinum wire coil or thin film on a ceramic substrate Typical resistance: 100 ohms at 0°C (Pt100 standard) Encountered in: Calibration Probe Fixture See also: Calibration Probe Fixture, Metrology

title: Screw Labeling
content: #Definitions Screw labeling refers to the standardized naming conventions used to describe the size, thread pitch, and length of threaded fasteners. There are two main systems used in engineering: Metric (ISO) and Unified National (imperial/inch-based). Both systems appear in equipment and fabrication work at Promega. In the metric system, the format is M[diameter] × [pitch] × [length]. For example, M3 × 0.5 × 10 means a metric screw with a 3 mm nominal outer diameter, a thread pitch of 0.5 mm (distance between threads), and a length of 10 mm. Length is typically measured from under the head for most head types. The pitch is sometimes omitted when it is the standard coarse pitch for that diameter — so "M3" alone generally implies M3 × 0.5. Fine-pitch variants (e.g. M3 × 0.35) are always called out explicitly. Common metric sizes encountered in equipment include M3, M4, M5, M6, and M8. In the Unified National (imperial) system, the format is [diameter]-[TPI] × [length]. For number sizes (#0–#12), the diameter is given as a screw number defined by ASME (specifically ASME B18.6.3 for machine screws). The nominal diameter in inches is calculated as: diameter = (N × 0.013) + 0.060, where N is the screw number. So a #4 screw has a nominal diameter of (4 × 0.013) + 0.060 = 0.112 inches (~2.8 mm). For example, 6-32 × 1/2 means a #6 diameter screw (~3.5 mm), 32 threads per inch, and 1/2 inch long. Above #12, fractional inch diameters are used — for example, 1/4-20 × 3/4 means 1/4 inch diameter, 20 TPI, and 3/4 inch long. Common imperial sizes encountered include 4-40, 6-32, 8-32, 10-32, and 1/4-20. Coarse thread (e.g. 6-32) is standard; fine thread (e.g. 6-40) appears occasionally on precision equipment. Head type and drive type are not encoded in the size label but are typically specified alongside it when ordering or calling out a fastener. Common head types include Socket Head Cap Screw (SHCS), button head, flat head (countersunk), and pan head. Common drive types include hex/Allen, Phillips, Torx, and slotted. [!info]- Details & Notes Unknowns: Length measurement convention can vary by head type — flat/countersunk heads measure total length; most others measure from under the head. Worth confirming when precision matters. See also: Fabrication

title: SolidWorks Drawing Standards
content: #Definitions Promega's internal standards for SolidWorks 2D engineering drawings. Compiled from Promega drawing guidelines and feedback from Brian during Mixer Chuck drawing review (2026-04-21). Use this note as a reference when creating or reviewing any drawing before ePDM check-in. Templates Use the correct Promega template based on units and part size. Template Units Sheet Size When to Use A - Inch Inches A (8.5 x 11") Small or simple inch parts B - Inch Inches B (11 x 17") Standard inch parts — most common A - Metric Millimeters A (8.5 x 11") Small or simple metric parts B - Metric Millimeters B (11 x 17") Standard metric parts If the drawing feels cramped on A size, move to B. Do not squeeze dimensions to fit. Title Block All fields are required unless noted. Description and material must be ALL CAPS. Field Requirement Equipment # Must be filled in (e.g. 134634) SAP Material # Must be filled in (e.g. 1016437) Drawing # Must match SAP Material # exactly Description ALL CAPS (e.g. CHUCK, MIXER, STYLE A) Material Full material name in ALL CAPS (e.g. 316L STAINLESS STEEL, POLYCARBONATE, DELRIN) Finish Required (e.g. AS MACHINED, ANODIZED) Drawn By Engineer initials Approved By Reviewer initials — must not be blank Date MM/DD/YYYY format Sheet Count X OF Y — verify total is accurate Rev Rev 1 or Rev A — never blank Scale Must NOT be called out — scale notation is obsolete at Promega General Tolerance .X ±.02 .XX ±.015 .XXX ±.005 ANGLES ±1° Multiple equipment numbers: if a part is used across multiple pieces of equipment, list all equipment numbers in the Notes section (top left of title block area), not in the Equipment # field. Notes Section The Notes section is required on every drawing. Minimum content: Note 1: LABEL PARTS WITH DWG NO. ENGRAVE OR LASER ETCH IN INDICATED POSITION Note 2: (add as needed for material callouts, special processes, etc.) Note 3: BREAK ALL SHARP EDGES Add an engraving location callout directly on the part view (leader pointing to the face or location where the drawing number should be etched). This corresponds to Note 1. If tiny chamfer dimensions are present only to break sharp edges, remove them and let Note 3 cover it instead. Stock parts: add a note specifying the vendor part number (e.g. Note 4: STOCK PART — McMaster P/N XXXXXXXX). Views Front view is the primary view and should show the most features Isometric placed in upper right — reference only, not dimensioned Every included view must show unique information not visible in another view — remove blank or redundant views Views must be properly aligned (top view directly above front, right view directly right) Section views: labeled SECTION A-A, cutting plane line shown on parent view Detail views: labeled DETAIL A, SCALE 2:1 with a circle and letter on the parent view Partial/relative views: labeled PARTIAL VIEW — [FACE NAME] Crop view borders must not appear on the printed drawing — check File > Print Preview before submitting Dimensioning General Rules Every feature must have a dimension — nothing implied or assumed All dimensions from one datum corner — do not mix datum origins mid-drawing All holes dimensioned from a single datum edge — do not chain-dimension hole to hole Use the SolidWorks Hole Callout tool for all hole callouts — not Smart Dimension Overall length, width, and height must all be explicitly called out No dimension should require math to use — all values directly readable No duplicate dimensions Leader lines must not cross each other — use detail views if needed Dimensions placed outside the part boundary — do not clutter the interior Chamfers: .XXX X 45° format Radii: R.XXX TYP when all equal (not called out on each corner separately) Diameter vs. Radius Show diameters on rectangular (profile) views where possible — not on circular/end views Replace radius dimensions with diameter dimensions where the center point is not physically accessible on the real part Cylinder with a Flat (Set Screw Flat) Dimension the flat depth from the bottom of the cylindrical surface, not from the center axis. The center is not measurable on the physical part. Tolerances Add tolerances to all diameters that have fit or function requirements. Use +0.001 / -0.000 style (unilateral) for bearing surfaces and press fits. General tolerance block covers everything else. Bearing Surfaces Add a surface finish callout and tight tolerances to any bearing surface. Confirm finish symbol and tolerance values with the machinist if unsure. Snap Ring Grooves Add a detail view showing the groove geometry — dimension one side of the groove only in the detail view. Confirm correct shaft diameter from the snap ring manufacturer data sheet before finalizing. Check for shaft diameter consistency on either side of the groove. Tapered Features Dimension: opening diameter, inside diameter, and angle. Do not dimension the tapered section length — it is not directly measurable. Repeated Features Use TYP callouts to avoid repeating identical dimensions. Example: R0.06 TYP for equal fillets, or 4X for hole patterns. Hole Callout Formats All hole callouts require a multiplier prefix (2X, 4X, etc.) even for single holes (1X). Hole Type Format Example Through hole (clearance) NX Ø[dia] THRU ALL 2X Ø0.201 THRU ALL Blind hole NX Ø[dia] ▽[depth] 4X Ø0.197 ▽0.590 Tapped through NX [thread spec] THRU ALL 4X M6x1.0 - 6H THRU ALL Tapped blind NX [thread spec] ▽[thread depth] Ø[drill dia] ▽[drill depth] 4X M6x1.0 - 6H ▽0.470 Ø0.197 ▽0.590 Counterbored NX Ø[dia] THRU ALL ⌴ Ø[cbore dia] ▽[cbore depth] 8X Ø6.600 THRU ALL ⌴ Ø14.550 ▽4.380 Countersunk NX Ø[dia] THRU ALL ∨ Ø[csink dia] X [angle]° 4X Ø0.201 THRU ALL ∨ Ø0.375 X 82° Multi-Sheet Drawings Name each sheet by its content (e.g. Outside Dimensions / Hole Positions / Flat Pattern) Add cross-reference notes on each sheet (e.g. SEE SHEET 2 FOR HOLE DIMENSIONS) No dimension duplicated across sheets All title blocks show correct X OF Y Curve-heavy parts: separate sheets per axis (Vertical / Horizontal / Arc Centers) Dense dimension areas: use Detail Views at 2:1 rather than squeezing overlapping leaders SAP material number note: the SAP Material number is always the parent drawing number. Sub-parts on multi-sheet drawings use letter suffixes on the drawing (e.g. 1016459A, 1016459B) but the SAP Material number stays as the parent (1016459). Sheet Metal Bend angle shown as interior angle (e.g. 45°, not 135°) Bend radius must be greater than or equal to material thickness Bend note format: UP 45° R0.03 (direction + angle + radius, all present) Bend notes generated by SolidWorks tool (Show Sheet Metal Bend Notes checked in view properties) Either flat or formed view marked (REF) — not both If cut from flat blank: formed view is (REF) If formed dimensions are critical: flat pattern is (REF) Flat pattern on Sheet 2 with overall flat dimensions and bend lines shown Hole callout must appear on the flat pattern sheet Stock Parts If a part is purchased stock (unmodified or modified): Unmodified stock: all dimensions shown as reference only (parenthesis oval style). Show OD, total thickness, and ID as a minimum. No modification dimensions. Add stock note with vendor part number. Modified stock: show stock dimensions as reference (parenthesis oval). Show modification dimensions as fully dimensioned with tolerances. Add stock note with vendor part number. Common Return-to-Engineer Errors Error Fix Rev field blank Must have Rev 1 or Rev A Scale called out Remove entirely Description not ALL CAPS Fix in title block Approved By blank Required before ePDM check-in Notes section blank Add Note 1 (engraving) and Note 3 (break sharp edges) at minimum Drawing number does not match SAP number Must be identical Overall dimension missing Explicitly call out total L, W, and H Holes chain-dimensioned Dimension all holes from single datum edge Hole callout missing multiplier prefix Add 1X, 2X, 4X, etc. Diameter shown on circular view Move to rectangular/profile view Radius used where center is inaccessible Replace with diameter Flat measured from center axis Measure from bottom of cylindrical surface Bend angle shows obtuse angle Change to interior angle Bend radius less than material thickness Minimum equals thickness Neither flat nor formed view marked REF One must be (REF) Crossing leader lines Use detail views or rearrange dimensions Partial or relative view has no label Add PARTIAL VIEW — [FACE NAME] Crop view border visible on print Check File > Print Preview and fix Stock part with no stock note Add Note with vendor part number Tiny chamfer dims present for sharp edge break only Remove and cover with Note 3 [!info]- Details & Notes Sources: Promega internal drawing guidelines; Brian drawing review session, 2026-04-21 See also: SolidWorks, ePDM, Mixer Chuck Drawing Review 1, Brian Osterbrink

title: MA3 Pusher
content: The MA3 Pusher is a two-part mechanical assembly at the end of the MA3 line. After cartridges are advanced along the line by the rake, the pusher advances pairs of two Maxwell RSC cartridges simultaneously into alignment zones on the Drop Gate, where they are loaded into trays. Once all trays (approximately 8, unverified) are loaded, they are suctioned up and stacked at the end of the line. The accuracy of the pusher's lateral position is critical — any offset translates directly into misalignment at the gate. Active redesign work is tracked in MA3 Pusher Redesign. #Definitions Assembly The pusher is a stainless steel assembly consisting of two main parts joined by two screws. The fork is a 3-4 mm thick stainless steel plate with a slight stepped profile, suggesting it was fully machined from 7-8 mm stock rather than formed from sheet metal. On the underside, there is a recessed rectangle that fits around the pneumatic base it mounts to, creating a lip that holds it in place. A pin on the base mates with a matching hole in the fork to lock its rotational position. A bolt hole on the base mates with a slotted hole on the fork, allowing slight adjustment for tolerance. The fork extends out and splits into two prongs, each with a countersunk hole to accept the mounting screws. The pusher plate is a small rectangular piece with holes that match the countersunk holes on the fork. The two parts are fastened flush using flat head screws. The face of the plate that contacts the cartridges is flat, with two vertical slots that align with a small plastic tab on the Maxwell RSC cartridges. The top of the plate has a slope to it, and the bottom is flush with the fork along the push plane. The original screws are believed to have been ordered by Optima, the vendor who manufactured MA3. The closest known equivalent is a 4-40 stainless steel flat head screw (McMaster-Carr 91781A106). [!info]- Details & Notes Unknowns: Number of trays loaded per cycle (~8, unverified). Original screw spec from Optima not confirmed. Encountered in: MA3 Pusher Redesign See also: MA3, Drop Gate, Maxwell RSC, Optima, Engineering Study

title: MA3
content: MA3 (MaxAuto3) is one of the MaxAuto fill/finish lines in the Feynman building. Like the other MaxAuto lines, it fills, seals, and labels cartridges used in Promega's Maxwell® RSC instrument kits. #Lines Known Equipment: Filling station Side print unit Camera (upstream) Rake Pusher Active Projects: Engineering Study — New pusher design (I will be involved in this project) MA2 software improvements being ported to MA3 by Bethany Zepnick (scheduled Friday 2026-03-27). Includes fixing mislabeled HMI buttons, a vacuum pump that never shuts off, and a crash issue when rejecting all objects. Notes: Cartridges on MA3 do not travel on a conveyor belt. Instead, they sit in slots on a rake — a comb-like mechanism that runs in sections along the cartridge path. The rake operates on a CAM cycle: it lifts the cartridges up, moves them forward one step, sets them down, then returns to its original position. This repeats continuously, advancing pairs of two cartridges through each station on the line (filling, foil, labeling, sealing, etc.). At the end of the line, the pusher takes over and advances the cartridges to the Drop Gate and tray stacker. MA3 has had more communication issues than MA2, potentially related to firmware version differences. A firmware upgrade (65400 series) was discussed in the P5 meeting (2026-03-25) but specifics are unresolved. Applied torque target is 26 in-lb; removal torque measured at ~21 in-lb (~81% of applied). This is within expected range. The torque range spec (24.5–27 in-lb) may need to be revisited since the set point does not match actual applied torque despite positive feedback from the machine. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: MaxAuto, MA1, MA2, Maxwell RSC, MA3 Pusher Redesign, HMI

title: Agitator
content: #Definitions An agitator is the mechanical mixing component inside a fermenter vessel. It spins to stir the culture, which serves two main purposes: distributing Dissolved Oxygen evenly throughout the liquid, and keeping the bacteria and nutrients suspended rather than settling. The agitator is driven by a motor and passes through the vessel wall via a mechanical seal — this seal is a critical sterile boundary point and is the first thing sterilized via SIP before each run, because it is the most vulnerable path for contamination to enter. Agitator speed (RPM) is one of the parameters set in the Batch recipe for each fermentation phase. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Fermenters, Sterilize in Place, Dissolved Oxygen, Batch

title: Batch Run Testing
content: #My-Projects Batch run testing is the process of verifying that the Batch recipe system is configured correctly for each OP and UP before real fermentation runs are executed. The goal is to walk through every step in the recipe, confirm that the correct prompts appear, the correct parameters appear, and that the overall flow is working as expected. Issues found are logged in Smartsheet, which acts as a shared bug list accessible to a small team at Cybertrol, the external contractor helping set up Batch on the fermenters. Workflow To start a test run, you open the batch editor, go to Batch View, then go to Batches and click Create New. This presents a list of options — these appear to be pre-built OPs or UP templates — from which you select the one you want to test (e.g. 3000L OP Biomass). A parameter list then appears, which you fill out. During batch testing, no physical process is running, so parameters are filled with unique but arbitrary values (e.g. sequential numbers 1, 2, 3, 4...) just to satisfy the input fields. Once started, you navigate to the P3 Control tab, then the P3 Fermentor Detail tab, and open the P3 Phases window. As the batch runs, prompts appear sequentially — including prompts to acknowledge hand valve positions. Since no physical equipment is running during testing, hand valves are set to a safe position beforehand and the prompts are acknowledged without actually checking them. You work through all the OPs and UPs for the vessel being tested, checking each individual step. Successful run paths are tracked by printing out the process trees and highlighting the completed paths. Notes on P3 Testing The first batch run testing done was on the 3000L P3 fermenter, starting with the 3000L OP Biomass. Multiple UPs and OPs for P3 were completed in the first session. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Active project as of early 2025 See also: Batch, Fermenters, Cybertrol, Smartsheet, HMI

title: Biomass
content: #Definitions Biomass refers to the total mass of living organisms growing in a fermenter at any given point — in Promega's context, this usually means bacteria. The biomass phase is the period after Inoculation where the organism is allowed to grow and multiply. Operators monitor growth over time and take samples to track progress. The goal is to reach a sufficient quantity of organism before either harvesting directly or proceeding to Induction to trigger protein expression. In multi-vessel recipes, biomass is grown in smaller vessels first and then transferred into a larger vessel to continue growing before harvest. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Inoculation, Induction, Fermenters

title: Cell Paste
content: #Definitions Cell paste is the solid fraction recovered after centrifugation during fermentation harvest. When the culture is spun in the centrifuges, the bacteria (cells) are separated from the liquid they were growing in. The cells pellet out into a thick paste. Most of Promega's fermentation products are expressed inside the cells, so the cell paste is the valuable material — it gets frozen on-site (sometimes for up to a year) or shipped to a downstream facility like RFC for further processing such as protein purification. The liquid fraction left over after centrifugation is called the Supernatant. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Centrifuge, Supernatant, RFC

title: Centrifuge
content: #Definitions Centrifuges are used in the fermentation harvest process to separate cell paste from the culture liquid (supernatant) after growth is complete. At Promega there are 8 centrifuges on the harvest line. They spin at approximately 17,000 RPM and are described as very large — roughly as tall as a bus. Culture is pushed into them from the fermenter using air pressure, and pumps at the centrifuges control the flow rate. Flow rate determines separation efficiency. Harvest lines running from the fermenters to the centrifuges must be sterilized via SIP before use. After centrifugation, the resulting cell paste or supernatant is either frozen on-site or shipped to a downstream facility like RFC for further processing. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Fermenters, Sterilize in Place, Cell Paste, Supernatant, RFC

title: Dissolved Oxygen
content: #Definitions Dissolved oxygen (DO) refers to the amount of oxygen present in solution in the fermenter culture. Most bacteria used in fermentation require oxygen to grow, so maintaining adequate dissolved oxygen is critical during the Biomass phase. Agitation helps distribute oxygen evenly throughout the vessel. Operators can choose to actively control dissolved oxygen to a setpoint (using agitation speed, airflow, or both) or simply supply air and let DO float — the choice depends on the organism and product being grown. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Biomass, Agitator, pH

title: Fermentation 101
content: #My-Projects Fermentation 101 was an introductory walkthrough of the Fermentation process at Promega, given to me by Misha in the Arnold Center. It covered the full order of operations from media prep and sterilization through biomass growth, induction, and harvest — as well as the control systems (Batch, operating procedures, unit procedures) used to run and sequence fermentation recipes. This note is a high-level summary; more detailed nodes branch off for individual concepts. The Purpose of Fermentation The goal of Fermentation is to grow one specific organism under controlled conditions. You need to monitor and tune several variables: pH (set to the organism's optimal range), dissolved oxygen, agitation (to distribute oxygen evenly), and various media and feed components. Once the organism has grown to the target amount, the product is harvested. Products can be expressed either inside the cell or outside the cell. Most of Promega's products are expressed inside the cells, but some are expressed externally. After harvest, cell paste or supernatant is either frozen on-site (sometimes for up to a year) or shipped to downstream facilities. One downstream facility is RFC, located out east off the Beltline — I likely won't go there. Another is an internal "supernatural batch" building. Roughly 3–4 batches produced here feed into 1 batch at RFC. One major product example: F884 (produced here) becomes E140 after protein purification — this is Luciferase, which feeds into a large number of Promega products. General Order of Operations Prepare media and load into the tank Sterilize — agitator seal, exhaust line, then the full vessel (SIP at 121°C for 15–30 min) Cool vessel to growth conditions Adjust pH, set dissolved oxygen control strategy Inoculate — introduce bacteria into the fermenter in a sterile way Monitor and allow biomass to grow Induction (if required) — change conditions once enough organism is present to trigger protein expression Sterilize harvest lines Transfer out to centrifuges using air pressure; pumps at the centrifuges control flow rate, which determines separation efficiency Freeze or ship the cell paste / supernatant Sterilization Detail Sterilization is done in phases, in order: Agitator seal SIP — the mechanical seal on the agitator is the most vulnerable sterile boundary and is sterilized first Exhaust SIP — the exhaust system at Promega connects back to a single shared tank, which creates unique challenges; the line must be sterilized before vessel sterilization Vessel SIP — every addition line going into the vessel must be sterilized; the entire vessel is brought to 121°C and held for 15–30 minutes, then cooled in a controlled manner. This is complex and involves manual hand valves. The concept of a "sterile boundary" is important — you're establishing a defined zone that is fully sterile, with every path crossing into that zone also sterilized. Multi-Vessel Recipes Some products require multiple vessels. The example discussed was FAFSAP, which uses P4/P5 (30 L), P1/P2 (300 L), and P3/P6 (3000 L), taking roughly 3 days start to finish. Certain media components will caramelize if sterilized together, so they're sterilized in separate vessels and then transferred in together. The process involves sterilizing each vessel, growing biomass in the smaller ones, sterilizing the transfer lines between them, and then transferring everything into the large harvest vessel before transferring out to the centrifuges. Control System Overview Fermentation is controlled and sequenced through a layered system in Batch: Phases — the atomic building blocks (e.g. seal SIP, exhaust SIP, vessel SIP, biomass, induction, transfer out). Each phase has parameters (temperature, agitation speed, pressure, etc.). Operating Procedures (OPs) — control and sequence phases directly; this is the link between the process control system and Batch Transitions (TS) — operator prompts between steps; you must confirm each transition to advance Unit Procedures — what recipes are actually built from; they sequence operating procedures together Batch — the top-level recipe system; captures parameter standards so products running repeatedly don't need to be reconfigured each run. Recipes can include parallel branches (e.g. simultaneously growing biomass while sterilizing addition lines from other vessels). The HMI is what operators use to interact with the system. EWS (Engineering Workstations) are the remote desktop servers used for engineering access. Phases List (in typical order) Media In Seal SIP Exhaust SIP Vessel SIP Biomass Induction Transfer In (from another vessel) Transfer Out SIP Transfer Out [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: This note is itself a project/learning note — the session with Misha on fermentation basics, held in the Arnold Center. See also: Fermentation, Arnold, Fermenters, Sterilize in Place, Centrifuge, Luciferase, Batch, EWS, HMI, RFC, Inoculation, Biomass, Induction, Cell Paste, Supernatant, Dissolved Oxygen, pH, Agitator, Fermentation Media

title: Fermentation Media
content: #Definitions Media (fermentation media) is the nutrient solution that bacteria grow in inside the fermenter. It contains everything the organism needs to survive and multiply — carbon sources, nitrogen sources, salts, vitamins, and other components depending on the organism and product. Media is loaded into the fermenter vessel before sterilization and is sterilized together with the vessel at Promega (some facilities sterilize them separately, but Promega sterilizes both together). In some recipes, additional feed components are added to the fermenter during the run through addition lines — these also need to have their lines sterilized via SIP before use. Certain media components cannot be sterilized together because they will caramelize at high temperatures; in multi-vessel recipes, those components are sterilized in separate vessels and combined afterward via transfer. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Fermenters, Steam in Place, Inoculation

title: Fermentation
content: #Definitions Fermentation is a biological manufacturing process used to grow a specific organism — typically bacteria — under tightly controlled conditions. The goal is to produce large quantities of a target organism or the protein it expresses, without contamination from any other organism. At Promega, fermentation takes place in the Arnold Center and is used to produce raw material (cell paste or supernatant) that feeds downstream purification and product manufacturing processes. Key variables monitored and controlled during fermentation include pH, dissolved oxygen, agitation speed, temperature, and the composition of media and feed components. The organism is grown in sealed, sterilized vessels called Fermenters. After reaching the target biomass, the culture is either harvested directly or first goes through Induction to express a target protein, then transferred to centrifuges for harvest. Products can be expressed inside the cell (most common at Promega) or outside the cell in the supernatant. After harvest, cell paste or supernatant is frozen on-site or shipped to downstream facilities like RFC for further processing. The fermentation process is controlled and sequenced using a recipe system called Batch, operated through HMIs and EWS workstations. [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Fermentation 101, Arnold, Fermenters, Sterilize in Place, Centrifuge, Batch, HMI, Luciferase, Inoculation, Biomass, Induction, Cell Paste, Supernatant, Dissolved Oxygen, pH, Agitator, Fermentation Media

title: Fermenters
content: #Definitions Fermenters are the sealed, sterilized vessels used in Fermentation to grow bacteria under controlled conditions. At Promega, fermenters come in three sizes. The 30-liter vessels are P4 and P5. The 300-liter vessels are P1 and P2. The 3000-liter vessels are P3 and P6. Each fermenter has an agitator with a mechanical seal (a key sterilization point), addition lines for adding media or feed components during a run, an exhaust line that connects back to a shared exhaust tank, and harvest lines that connect to the centrifuges. All of these lines and surfaces must be sterilized via SIP before use. Fermenters are controlled through the Batch recipe system. In multi-vessel recipes, smaller fermenters are used to grow initial biomass, which is then transferred into a larger vessel for the main fermentation and eventual harvest. Some media components cannot be sterilized together (they caramelize), so they are sterilized in separate vessels and transferred in together. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Sterilize in Place, Centrifuge, Batch, Agitator, Biomass, Fermentation Media

title: Induction
content: #Definitions Induction is a step in Fermentation where conditions inside the fermenter are deliberately changed to trigger the bacteria to express (produce) a specific target protein. It happens after the Biomass phase, once there is enough organism in the tank. The idea is to first grow a large population of bacteria under conditions optimized for growth, then switch to conditions that make those bacteria produce the desired product. Not all fermentation runs require an induction step — it depends on the product. At Promega, induction is one of the defined phases in the Batch recipe system and happens before harvest lines are sterilized and the transfer out to the centrifuges begins. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Biomass, Batch, Centrifuge

title: Inoculation
content: #Definitions Inoculation is the step in Fermentation where the target organism (typically bacteria) is introduced into the sterilized fermenter vessel in a sterile way. Before inoculation, the vessel has been sterilized via SIP, cooled to growth conditions, and adjusted to the correct pH. Once inoculated, the organism begins growing and the Biomass phase starts. Maintaining sterility during inoculation is critical — any contamination introduced at this step is very difficult to trace and eliminate. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Fermenters, Biomass, Sterilize in Place, pH

title: Sterilize in Place
content: #Definitions Sterilize in Place (SIP) is a sterilization method used to sterilize fermentation vessels and associated lines without disassembling them. The general concept is that sterilization is performed in place — the medium used to sterilize can vary, but at Promega it is done with steam, bringing the vessel or line up to 121°C and holding that temperature for at least 15–30 minutes before cooling back down in a controlled manner. At Promega's fermentation area in the Arnold Center, SIP is performed in a specific sequence for each vessel: the agitator seal is sterilized first (it is the most vulnerable sterile boundary), followed by the exhaust line, and then the full vessel. Every addition line and path going into the vessel must also be sterilized as part of the vessel SIP. The exhaust system connects back to a single shared tank, which adds complexity to exhaust SIP. All of this is managed using manual hand valves, which makes timing and safety important. SIP is one of the defined phases in the Batch fermentation recipe system. It also applies to harvest lines (sterilized before transfer out to the centrifuges) and to transfer lines between Fermenters in multi-vessel recipes. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Fermenters, Batch, Centrifuge, Agitator

title: Supernatant
content: #Definitions Supernatant is the liquid fraction that remains after centrifugation during fermentation harvest. When the culture is spun in the centrifuges, the cells pellet out as Cell Paste and the liquid above them is the supernatant. For products that are secreted outside the cell (rather than expressed inside), the supernatant is the valuable fraction. At Promega, most products are intracellular, so supernatant is less commonly the primary product — but it is still collected and may be processed depending on the product. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Centrifuge, Cell Paste

title: pH
content: #Definitions pH is a measure of how acidic or basic a solution is, on a scale from 0 to 14. A pH of 7 is neutral (pure water), below 7 is acidic, and above 7 is basic (alkaline). In Fermentation, pH is one of the key variables monitored and controlled. Every organism has an optimal pH range for growth, and straying too far outside that range can slow or stop growth entirely. Before Inoculation, the fermenter is adjusted to the target pH. It is then monitored and controlled throughout the run. [!info]- Details & Notes Encountered in: Fermentation 101 See also: Fermentation, Dissolved Oxygen, Biomass

title: Brain Dump Interviewer
content: #My-Projects The Brain Dump Interviewer is a Claude skill that conducts a conversational interview with a co-op to extract and document their accumulated workplace knowledge. It asks structured follow-up questions about terms, acronyms, people, places, equipment, software, and production lines — then outputs a clean, consistently formatted .txt file. That file is then fed into the New Hire Glossary Builder to be converted into Obsidian markdown notes. The skill uses a widget-driven interview flow, steering the conversation through categories without reciting them as a checklist. It extracts glossary items from project stories rather than documenting the engineering specifics of the projects themselves. Every entry in the output includes a "Heard in context of" field to preserve the source and conversational context for the parser downstream. The skill was created by Ryan and has been sent out to the other co-ops to gather more data for the Gizmos and Gadgets Glossary. How It Works Co-op starts a new chat and the skill triggers via keywords like "brain dump" or "document what I know" Claude interviews the co-op conversationally using widgets to steer and typed questions to extract detail At the end, Claude runs a quality check to catch any missed terms, names, or locations Output is a structured .txt file with sections for Definitions, People, Buildings, Lines, Manufacturing, Process Equipment, Software, My Projects, Other, and Unknown That .txt file is handed off to the New Hire Glossary Builder skill for processing into the vault Future Improvements Continue refining the interview flow based on feedback from co-ops Improve coverage of edge cases (e.g. entries that span multiple categories) [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Gizmos and Gadgets Glossary, New Hire Glossary Builder, New Hire Help Hub

title: Drawing From 3D Model
content: #My-Projects This is a potential collaboration with Jack Miller to test and help develop an AI tool that generates SolidWorks drawings automatically from 3D models. As of the biweekly product finishing huddle on 2026-03-23, the tool is consistently applying the correct drawing template, selecting the right views, and populating title block information. The main outstanding issue is dimensioning — it is not reliably placing dimensions, particularly on smaller parts, and sometimes skips them entirely rather than prompting for manual input. Jack and Ali Heinemann have both been working on this. The suggestion from the meeting was that I try the tool on my own projects to get more reps on it, and potentially collaborate with Jack directly — possibly sharing the same baseline so we can both work on solving the dimensioning problem together. [!info]- Details & Notes Unknowns: Exact tool/platform not confirmed (AI-assisted, likely Claude-based). Unclear whether Jack and Ali are working from the same codebase or independently. Encountered in: Biweekly product finishing team huddle, 2026-03-23 See also: Jack Miller, Ali Heinemann

title: Gizmos and Gadgets Glossary
content: #My-Projects The Gizmos and Gadgets Glossary is a shared knowledge base for Promega co-ops. It is designed to make common terms, acronyms, people, teams, and manufacturing lines easily accessible to new hires who are trying to get up to speed. The glossary lives as an Obsidian vault in Ryan's OneDrive, separate from his personal vault, and currently has roughly 10–30 entries. The long-term vision is to make it publicly accessible to all co-ops, though the delivery mechanism has not been decided yet. The glossary is populated using two Claude skills working in sequence: the Brain Dump Interviewer collects raw knowledge from co-ops through a conversational interview and outputs a structured .txt file, and the New Hire Glossary Builder parses those .txt files and converts them into individual Obsidian-ready markdown notes. Brain dump data has been sent out to the other co-ops and is still being collected. Current State Vault exists on Ryan's OneDrive in a separate folder from his personal vault Roughly 10–30 entries populated so far Waiting on brain dump submissions from other co-ops Future Goals Collect brain dump submissions from all co-ops and process them into the vault Make the glossary accessible to all co-ops once it has enough content — delivery method TBD Tie into the New Hire Help Hub as a key resource for incoming co-ops [!info]- Details & Notes See also: Brain Dump Interviewer, New Hire Help Hub, New Hire Glossary Builder

title: New Hire Help Hub
content: #My-Projects The New Hire Help Hub is a concept for a centralized SharePoint page that brings together different co-op-built tools and resources to help new hires get oriented at Promega. It is mostly still an idea at this stage — the vision is a combination of different co-op projects rather than a single tool built by one person. The Gizmos and Gadgets Glossary is expected to be one of the core resources linked from this hub. title: Teams Album of the Day

It is built entirely with Power Automate and Microsoft Forms — there is no Claude component. Each day it posts a card with a randomized greeting and an album submitted via the form. A startup guide exists as a .docx file (link TBD). title: Dip Tube Baffle Assembly
content: #My-Projects The Dip Tube Baffle Assembly project is a design and fabrication project done for Philip. He is collaborating with the IVD team on this project. The assembly is a 2L sinker shroud with two metal barbed hose connectors that needed redesign. The opposite side of the barb piece includes some kind of bubble protection feature (bubble catcher or releaser — exact function unclear). The barb pickup pieces needed to be redesigned to better fit two different hose sizes — one small and one large. The pieces also needed to be mirrored/centered, as the original part was off-center. The reference drawing for the original part is SAP #1015703, a 316L stainless steel 2L sinker shroud with 2 barb welds. Design History Produced two prototype barb pickup pieces based on the original drawing and a small plastic barb reference part: 4.8 mm tubing barb piece — sized based on an existing barb used elsewhere at Promega for 4.8 mm tubing 3.2 mm tubing barb piece — scaled from the 4.8 mm version (2/3 size) Both pieces included same-size bubble protection, with the 4.8 mm version scaled to 3/2 of the 3.2 mm side Total width target: <= 25 mm Printed 3 different sizes on the Formlabs Form 3L After a second meeting, redesigned to produce 3 more larger versions (slightly smaller than prototype 1, using intuition to determine sizing). Philip then met with the IVD team separately to select preferred sizes. They chose to move forward with the largest of both size options. Current Action Items (from Philip's email, 2/20/2026) Confirm both selected sizes are on the same barb piece that was 3D printed Send Philip the .prt and .dwg files for the updated barb piece (for machine shop submission) Open the existing assembly zip file and confirm the updated barb piece fits correctly with the other parts (dip tube and baffles) — parts will be fabricated separately and welded together Specifically check that the bottom of the dip tube pickup rod has the ~14–15 mm notch that's thinner — this is where the barb weld slides on when welded Create a new Pack and Go assembly (if that's the right term) with the .prt, .dwg, and .asm files — see Solidworks Generate PDFs of the barb piece and total assembly — reference the two attached drawings for dimensions to call out Notes Philip mentioned a future mini project involving designing a lid — not yet started. [!info]- Details & Notes Official name/model: SAP #1015703 — 2L Sinker Shroud 2 Barbs Weld (316L Stainless Steel) Unknowns: Exact function of the bubble protection feature not confirmed. "Pack and Go" assembly term unverified — Philip's phrasing, may need to confirm correct SolidWorks term. Encountered in: Collaboration with Philip McKeefry and the IVD team See also: Philip McKeefry, In Vitro Dispensing, Formlabs Form 3L, Solidworks

title: Falling Vial Bin
content: #My-Projects ⚠️ HIGH PRIORITY — flagged in 1:1 with Ali on 2026-04-03. Get this off the list. Status Waiting for Paul to update me on testing results from 03/02/26. Can make sheet metal ramp or other modifications if necessary. Once confirmed, I will do parts process Background Vials from the Lyo go through an intermediate room before the go to the finishing station. If a vial is on its side, it will roll off the conveyor into a bin. This bin has a sloped bottom and a hole on the low end so that it could potentially be funneled into another conveyor and through the wall, but that will not be implemented. Paul is having me patch the hole on the one end, since their temporary fix was just cardboard and tape. It also has the vials falling a significant distance, so a ramp to slow the vials down will be implemented. Process I will use Solidworks to model current bin using the sheet metal features in order to get reference geometry for the added features. This will be done by first creating the general shape as a solid body, using the shell feature to bring it to the sheet metal gauge, and then converting to sheet metal. To cap the end, I made a drawing on a sticky note of a possible solution. It is only 1 bend, and is mostly a flat rectangle with a flange to mount it to existing screw holes in the bin. I will make sure that there are no gaps big enough to let the smallest vials through. For the ramp, my current design is a straight ramp that does not go all the way to the top, allowing a couple inches to fall. This will allow more volume, but reduce the falling distance some. This will be easier to fold at Lamarr or DaVinci. Both parts could be cut manually, but cutting them on the water jet first might be better for the precise angles. 02/28/26 I manually cut the patched hole using the shear, then drew out lines to cut corners out on square press (?), then drilled holes on the drill press, and folded on the finger break. Action Items - Do two-page drawing: one overall assembly drawing (no dimensions) and one patch drawing (with dimensions). Ask Paul about part number approach — the bin existed previously without a PN, and a new part was made to modify it. Need to clarify whether there are two separate PNs, one PN for the whole assembly, or something else entirely before starting the parts process.

title: Gel Electrophoresis Tray
content: #My-Projects Status & Remaining Work Priority (as of 2026-04-10): #4 of active projects Most of the tray components have been cut. The joint design has been updated to use a rabbet joint for cleaner, stronger epoxy bonds. Solidworks models need to be updated to reflect this before machining continues. Remaining work: Update Solidworks models to incorporate rabbet joint on all mating surfaces Update 2D drawings to reflect rabbet joint geometry Machine updated parts and do test fit Cut combs and comb handles Epoxy tray together and check fit and stability Redesign and resize sealed wall — the O-ring cord didn't fit well in the current version Get SAP part numbers assigned Fill out parts process forms (CoC and ePDM) Meet with Zack to review this project alongside the 96 Well Bracket project Make any changes from Zack's feedback, produce final parts, complete parts process Update Nikki on the amount made (for inventory) Zack Wermeling asked me to fabricate a new gel electrophoresis tray. The tray is made of polycarbonate (acrylic was also considered). All pieces are cut at Lamarr and epoxied together into their final assemblies. Tray Components The tray itself is made of 3 unique parts that get epoxied together: Base — a flat rectangle with two groove slots that accept the sealed walls. Side Walls — the long walls of the tray, with slots and grooves for both the combs and the sealed walls. Top Lip — follows the perpendicular shape of the side walls and acts as a lip that slots into the holder machine. Sealed Walls The sealed walls are based on a previous tray Zack gave me as reference. They use an O-ring cord lining the edges to create a watertight seal, and function as the two end walls of the gel tray. Currently Zack's team just tapes off the ends — the sealed walls are the improved solution. The base and side walls have grooves machined in to accept them. Combs There are two comb variants: a 16-tooth comb and a 1-tooth comb (the 1-tooth is just a single large central tooth). Each comb is two parts epoxied together: Tooth plate — the thin flat piece with the actual teeth. Handle/body — a thicker piece that slots into the tray and acts as a handle. They are two separate pieces for manufacturing purposes but become one assembly after epoxying. Fabrication Notes Material is polycarbonate, sized to standard PC thicknesses already stocked at Lamarr to avoid needing to face anything down. This was a design adjustment made early on — originally parts were sized for smoothness/clarity thinking DNA contact mattered, but Zack confirmed only the gel contacts the tray so that wasn't necessary. Most cuts are done on the Zund. The Zund struggles with very thin plastic, so the combs will be cut differently — the plan is to face a sheet of PC on the Zund first, then use the Laser Cutter for the fine comb cuts. Part Numbers SAP Material # Description Order Qty BOM Equipment # BOM Qty 1016469 Tray, Gel Electrophoresis, Polycarbonate 2 113596 1 1016470 Comb, Gel Tray, Polycarbonate, 16-Tooth 2 113596 1 1016475 Comb, Gel Tray, Polycarbonate, 1-Tooth 2 113596 1 1016480 Wall, Gel Tray, Polycarbonate, Sealed 4 113596 1 Note: Tray (1016469) contains 3 sub-part numbers (PN-A, PN-B, PN-C) for its different subcomponents — to be assigned. title: Label Arm
content: #My-Projects Paul D needed a fix to an issue they have had in their vial labeler. It would shoot out vials, so an arm was created to hold them in place. This arm would sometimes cause the vials to lean at an angle and the labels would be applied askew. To fix this, I was tasked with modifying the arm to hold the vial on both sides in hopes to fix this issue. Created Dual Arm model. Future Notes Currently Flimsy made of plastic, maybe try stiffer plastic and FDM for future prototypes Shorten arms by about 1.5 inches (Unnecessarily long) Remove inner screw hole Move bridge over to inner screw hole edge for extra support and to reduce bending Raise everything slightly (Thicken the mounting plate? Move the second arm shoulder inwards (See picture) Retain spacing with other arm, so bring other arm the same direction slightly.

title: MA3 Pusher Redesign
content: #My-Projects Action Items Check with Matt about what improvements he has in mind for the pusher (flagged in 1:1, 2026-04-10) Verify whether the slots on the front plate are still needed (flagged in 1:1, 2026-04-10) Investigate fine adjustment mechanism for pusher plate position Shift front plate left by ~1-2 mm in model to correct alignment offset Verify pin hole diameter in model (may be undersized — required filing during fit test) Fix countersink dimensions in model (screw sitting slightly proud) Confirm whether plate offset is a model error or 3D print warping from transport Print updated prototype on Markforged Industrial X7 once model is corrected Print pusher (3D print test candidate — flagged in 1:1, 2026-04-03) Steps Step 1: Fix Offset Issue Resolve the sideways translation (offset) issue with the pusher. See Prototype Fit Test below for findings. Step 2: Design Options for the End Develop design options for the pusher end. 3D print and test candidates. Step 3: Drop Gate Improvements Evaluate how lead-ins on the Drop Gate can be modified to improve function. Begin with a 3D print. Ryan leads Change Control for this step. Step 3a: Assess whether the connection point can be modified to allow for future adjustments, avoiding the need to redesign from scratch later. Prototype Fit Test — 2026-04-10 (ECR) A prototype was printed in Markforged Onyx on the industrial X7 at Lamarr. The original design was modeled in Solidworks from measurements of the existing part. The redesign removes the bend/curve from the original fork while maintaining the front plate slot geometry. No changes were made to the mounting interface. The screws used for the test were 4-40 stainless steel flat head hex screws (McMaster-Carr 91781A106). The originals are believed to have been sourced by Optima, the vendor who built MA3, but the exact spec is unconfirmed. The McMaster screws were the closest available equivalent. Note: some or all of the dimensional errors below may be attributable to polymer expansion/contraction during 3D printing rather than model inaccuracies. Issues Found Pin hole too small — the pin hole did not accept the mounting pin. The hole was filed open by hand to give enough clearance to seat the part. Could be a model error or a print shrinkage issue. Countersink too shallow — the flat head screws sit slightly proud of the surface rather than flush. Not a functional issue for the prototype but needs to be corrected in the model. Front plate misaligned — the plate slots are shifted approximately 1-2 mm to the right relative to the cartridge tabs. Pushing the plate face manually to the left brought the slots into alignment, confirming the offset is lateral and consistent. The plastic flexibility allowed this as a temporary check — slot spacing and slot width were confirmed good when manually corrected. The offset is likely a model measurement error, but warping from transport (prototype was carried in a backpack) is also possible. Reduced rigidity — the Onyx prototype is noticeably less rigid than the stainless steel original. This is expected given the material difference and is not a concern for the final machined part, but it affected how the prototype behaved during the fit test. Raw Notes from ECR "The pin hole is too small. Could be issue with plastic, but the slots are slightly to the right. Pushing the end face to the left aligns them correctly. Estimated shift: 1-2 mm." Long-Term Plan Evaluate model options, including 3D printed versions Conduct Engineering Study (Ryan leads) — a structured internal process with informal steps (active as of 2026-03-20) Narrow down design options Machine final part Complete Change Control to add final part: EPDM updates CoC Final parts process
